title: Writing - Part 2: Exercise 1
description: Choose one of the following writing tasks and write your answer in 220-260 words.

tasks.title: Report
tasks.prompt: A national digital inclusion taskforce has commissioned a report on the barriers preventing elderly residents from accessing online public services. Your report should identify the main barriers, assess the consequences of digital exclusion for this demographic, and recommend practical measures to improve digital inclusion for older adults.
tasks.modelAnswer:
**Report on Digital Exclusion Among Elderly Residents: Barriers and Recommendations**

**Introduction**
This report identifies the principal barriers preventing elderly residents from accessing online public services, assesses the consequences of their exclusion, and sets out practical recommendations for improving digital inclusion. Evidence is drawn from national surveys, focus groups with residents aged sixty-five and above, and consultation with third-sector digital inclusion organisations.

**Main Barriers**
Three categories of barrier emerge consistently. Skills and confidence: many older adults lack foundational digital literacy and express significant anxiety about making mistakes online, particularly in relation to financial transactions and personal data. Device access: a substantial proportion of those aged seventy-five and above do not own a smartphone or computer. Connectivity: low fixed-income households are disproportionately likely to lack home broadband, relying on limited mobile data or forgoing internet access entirely.

**Consequences of Exclusion**
As government services migrate to digital-first delivery, digitally excluded residents face increased difficulty accessing benefits, healthcare appointments, council services and utilities management. This creates additional demand on already stretched telephone and in-person service channels, and disproportionately disadvantages those with the greatest need.

**Recommendations**
Three measures are recommended: expanding publicly funded digital skills training through libraries, GP surgeries and community centres; providing subsidised device loans for income-qualified households; and requiring all public-facing digital services to maintain accessible non-digital equivalents as a legal standard.

**Conclusion**
Digital inclusion cannot be achieved through technology provision alone; it requires sustained, community-embedded support and a commitment to maintaining non-digital access for those who need it.

tasks.title: Email
tasks.prompt: You are a volunteer coordinator for a digital inclusion charity. You have learned that a large technology company in your city is planning a corporate social responsibility initiative and is seeking local charity partners. Write an email to the company's CSR manager explaining your charity's work with digitally excluded communities, outlining a partnership proposal that would make use of the company's resources and expertise, and inviting them to visit your centre.
tasks.modelAnswer:
Dear Ms Lindqvist,

I am writing on behalf of Connected Communities, a charity that has been supporting digitally excluded residents across the north of the city for the past seven years. Having read about TechCore's forthcoming CSR initiative, I believe there is a compelling opportunity for a mutually beneficial partnership that would make a tangible difference to some of the most disadvantaged households in our area.

Connected Communities currently runs weekly digital skills workshops from three community centres, serving approximately two hundred and forty participants per month. The majority are older adults, low-income families and recent migrants who lack the skills, devices or connectivity required to access essential online services — from Universal Credit and NHS appointment booking to job applications and utility management. Demand for our services consistently exceeds our capacity, and we have a waiting list of over one hundred and fifty people.

I would like to propose a partnership in which TechCore contributes in three specific ways. First, by donating refurbished devices from your corporate refresh cycles to our device lending library. Second, by offering pro bono time from TechCore employees to co-facilitate our intermediate and advanced skills workshops, bringing expertise our volunteer team does not currently possess. Third, by funding a part-time digital inclusion coordinator post, which would allow us to extend our reach into two additional communities currently unserved.

I would very much welcome the opportunity to show you our work in person. Would you be available to visit our Northgate centre in the coming weeks?

With warm regards,
Frances Mbeki
Volunteer Coordinator, Connected Communities

tasks.title: Review
tasks.prompt: You have recently read a book called 'Offline: The Hidden Cost of the Digital Revolution' which argues that the rapid digitalisation of public services, commerce and social life is systematically disadvantaging those without reliable access to technology. Write a review for a technology and society journal, evaluating the strength of the book's arguments, the quality of its evidence, and whether it offers practical pathways forward.
tasks.modelAnswer:
**Urgent, Well-Evidenced and Productively Uncomfortable: A Review of 'Offline: The Hidden Cost of the Digital Revolution'**

In 'Offline', sociologist Dr Miriam Osei-Bonsu delivers a carefully argued and frequently unsettling examination of what she terms 'digital solutionism' — the tendency of governments, corporations and institutions to adopt digital-first approaches to service delivery without adequate consideration of those unable to participate.

The book's central argument is persuasive and well-evidenced: as public services, healthcare, employment and civic participation migrate to digital platforms, those without connectivity, devices or digital literacy do not merely experience inconvenience; they are structurally excluded from the mechanisms through which contemporary societies distribute resources and opportunity. Osei-Bonsu marshals an impressive range of quantitative data — from NHS Digital figures on patients who missed appointments due to an inability to use the online booking system to DWP data on benefit claimants who failed to complete digital applications — alongside richly textured qualitative accounts from affected individuals.

The book is particularly strong in its refusal to treat digital exclusion as a temporary transitional problem that will resolve itself as younger, digitally native generations age through the population. The structural factors — poverty, disability, geographic isolation, language barriers — that produce digital exclusion today are not inherently generational.

The final section on policy recommendations is, to the author's credit, concrete rather than aspirational, offering detailed proposals grounded in existing international examples.

This is an important, timely book that deserves a wide readership among technologists, policymakers and concerned citizens alike.
